did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep convolutional neural networks (specifically improved InceptionV3, ResNet50, and DenseNet201 models based on transfer learning) to classify 7 types of skin lesions from the ISIC 2017 HAM10000 dataset. The best model (improved ResNet50) achieved an accuracy of 86.69%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and implementation of improved deep convolutional neural networks (InceptionV3, ResNet50, DenseNet201) for skin lesion classification using the ISIC 2017 HAM10000 dataset. It includes sections on experimental design, data preprocessing, model training, and results analysis with specific accuracy metrics (e.g., 86.69% for improved ResNet50), which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesion Classification based on Deep Convolutional Neural Network
journal: 2022 IEEE 4th International Conference on Power, Intelligent Computing and Systems (ICPICS)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer diagnosis by developing improved deep convolutional neural networks for classifying seven types of skin lesions using the ISIC 2017 HAM10000 dataset. The authors employed transfer learning to modify InceptionV3, ResNet50, and DenseNet201 architectures, incorporating new pooling and fully connected layers while fine-tuning parameters pre-trained on ImageNet. Data preprocessing included image cropping, hair removal, and balancing techniques such as under-sampling and oversampling to handle class imbalance. The models were evaluated on a dataset partitioned into 80% training and 20% testing sets, with performance measured by classification accuracy. The experimental results demonstrated that the improved ResNet50 model achieved the highest accuracy of 86.69%, outperforming the other modified architectures and existing methods. The study concludes that these improved deep learning models offer a reliable and accurate approach for automated skin lesion classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 7 classes in the ISIC 2017 HAM10000 dataset; had the largest sample size (6707) before under-sampling to 1006.

Listed as one of the 7 categories in the ISIC 2017 HAM10000 dataset description, though specific sample counts for this class were not detailed in Table I.

all_task_types.target: 7 types of skin lesions (melanocytic nevus, melanoma, benign keratosis lesions, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, vascular lesions, and dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the ISIC 2017 HAM10000 dataset. The models (improved InceptionV3, ResNet50, and DenseNet201) predict discrete diagnostic categories. Performance is measured by classification accuracy, with the improved ResNet50 achieving 86.69% accuracy. The output layer uses a Softmax activation function for classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopy images of skin lesions from 7 diagnostic categories, covering various body positions such as the face and back. The original dataset contained 10,015 images.

use.training: Used for training the improved InceptionV3, ResNet50, and DenseNet201 models. 80% of the preprocessed and balanced samples (5826 images) were used as the training set.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate held-out validation set; the paper mentions monitoring test accuracy during training and shows validation accuracy/loss for a preliminary 3-layer CNN experiment, but the main transfer learning experiments used an 80/20 train/test split.
use.testing: Used for testing the models. 20% of the preprocessed and balanced samples (1457 images) were used as the test set.
use.other: The original dataset provided 10,015 images. The study selected 7,283 samples after preprocessing and applied data augmentation (under-sampling for pigmented nevi and oversampling for other classes) to balance the dataset before splitting.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy and loss functions.

ISIC 2017 HAM10000.experiment: Improved Deep Convolutional Networks based on Transfer Learning

Improved ResNet50.notes: Best performing model. Accuracy is 3% higher than comparable other methods.

ISIC 2017 HAM10000.notes: The improved ResNet50 achieved the highest test accuracy of 86.69%. The improved DenseNet201 had the highest validation accuracy (86.91%) and lowest training loss (0.0278), but ResNet50 was identified as the best model in the text. Table III in the paper lists the test accuracy for InceptionV3 as 85.80%, ResNet50 as 86.69%, and DenseNet201 as 86.91%.

architecture_used.main_model: Improved InceptionV3, Improved ResNet50, and Improved DenseNet201
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from ImageNet pre-trained weights

architecture_details.modifications: Removed the original output layer; added new pooling layer and fully connected layers; combined some original convolutional and pooling layers with the new layers to form improved models
architecture_details.layers: Modified versions of InceptionV3, ResNet50, and DenseNet201; also a preliminary 3-layer CNN with input layer, three pairs of convolutional/max pooling layers, and two fully connected layers

data_augmentation: Random horizontal and vertical offset amplitude
dataset_balancing: Oversampling (8x) for classes with few samples (Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesions, etc.) via random crop, flip, translate

preprocessing_techniques: Image Cropping: Original 600x450 images were cropped to 224x224 to remove edge information.
Hair Removal: Closing operation in mathematical morphology, grey processing, and binarization were used to remove hair interference.
Noise Removal: Techniques were applied to remove little noises during the hair removal process.
Data Augmentation (Amplification): Random flipping, random horizontal and vertical offset amplitude, shearing, and amplification were used to generate new image samples.
Dataset Balancing (Under-sampling): Pigmented nevus samples were under-sampled by random sampling with a reduction ratio of 0.15.
Dataset Balancing (Oversampling): Classes with few samples (dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, etc.) were oversampled 8 times using random crop, flip, and translate.
conclusions: The study concludes that deep convolutional neural networks improved with transfer learning can effectively classify seven types of skin lesions from the ISIC 2017 HAM10000 dataset.
Among the three improved architectures tested (InceptionV3, ResNet50, and DenseNet201), the improved ResNet50 model achieved the highest classification accuracy of 86.69%.
The improved ResNet50 model's accuracy was reported to be 3% higher than comparable existing methods.

The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed improved deep learning models offer a reliable and accurate approach for automated skin lesion classification, aiding in the auxiliary diagnosis of skin cancer.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single internal 80/20 train-test split of the ISIC 2017 HAM10000 dataset without external validation, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

Class imbalance was addressed via aggressive synthetic oversampling (8x) for minority classes (e.g., Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma) and under-sampling for the majority class (Pigmented Nevus), which may introduce bias and overfitting risks specific to the augmented training distribution.
The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for accuracy metrics, preventing statistical assessment of result stability or significance.
No formal hypothesis testing or p-values were provided to support the claim that the improved ResNet50's accuracy (86.69%) is significantly superior to comparable methods.

The claim that the model's accuracy is '3% higher than comparable other methods' lacks specific baseline comparisons with identical preprocessing and splitting protocols, making the magnitude of improvement difficult to verify.
The dataset is restricted to specific body positions (face, back) and may not represent the full diversity of skin lesion presentations across different anatomical sites or skin types.

As a conference paper, the methodological detail is limited compared to journal standards, and the hardware specifications (Intel i9, 64GB RAM) are reported but computational efficiency or inference time metrics are absent.
